risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We will use a permuted block algorithm with random block lengths to generate the randomization assignments." Comment: This explicitly describes a random allocation sequence generation method (permuted blocks with random block lengths), which meets the criteria for low risk of bias for random sequence generation.

Quote: "Randomization will be executed via a secure web-based data management system. To ensure masking of the assessment staff to intervention assignment, the randomization procedures will be performed by staff members not involved in the assessments." Comment: A secure web-based randomization system is an explicit allocation concealment mechanism that prevents recruiters/enrolling staff from foreseeing the next assignment, so allocation concealment is judged low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The study design is a single-site randomized controlled trial comparing telephone-delivered cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT-T) with nondirective supportive therapy (NST-T)" and "NST-T will be conducted by telephone via weekly 45–50 minute sessions with the randomly assigned therapist. ... The NST-T condition matches the CBT-T condition in terms of amount of therapist contact..." Comment: Participants and therapists/personnel delivering the intervention could not be blinded in this psychotherapy trial because the interventions were visibly different (CBT content vs supportive therapy), even though contact time was matched. Knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence the main outcomes (anxiety, worry, depression, quality of life), which are subjective and therapist/participant-reported or interviewer-mediated, so performance bias is at high risk overall.

Quote: "All interviewer-based measures are administered by telephone by a trained assessor who is blinded to treatment condition. Self-report measures are mailed to the participants with a stamped return envelope enclosed." Comment: The main interviewer-rated outcome (HAM-A) was assessed by a blinded assessor, and the study explicitly states masking of assessment staff to treatment assignment. The other key outcome (PSWQ-A) is self-report, so the participant is the assessor; however, the paper also notes interviewer-based measures were blinded and does not suggest any unblinded subjective outcome assessment by staff. Overall, for the interviewer-rated outcomes most relevant to detection bias, blinding was clearly in place, and the outcome measurement is unlikely to have been influenced by knowledge of allocation.

Quote: "In the primary analysis, all randomized subjects will be included in their original study group regardless of the final mode of intervention or the extent of compliance with the study protocol; that is, the primary analysis will follow an “intent to treat” philosophy." Comment: The paper describes an ITT approach with mixed-model repeated measures analysis for longitudinal outcomes, which is an appropriate method for handling some missing data. It also states the trial was planned for about 10% dropout, suggesting attrition was expected to be modest rather than large. No evidence in the provided methods indicates imbalanced or outcome-related missing data that would bias results.
